summary:
The Dian Kingdom (c. 300–109 BC) was a Bronze Age kingdom on the shores of Dian Lake in modern Yunnan province, China, whose extraordinary bronze art — depicting scenes of sacrifice, warfare, and daily life with remarkable vividness — was discovered in 1955 and revealed a sophisticated culture largely unknown to Chinese historiography.

The Dian people lived in the highlands of southwestern China, maintaining relative isolation from the major Chinese states to the north. Their bronze vessels and figurines, cast using lost-wax techniques, show a culture with complex religious practices, hierarchical society, and a rich material culture connected to both Chinese traditions and Southeast Asian influences. The historian Sima Qian mentions the Dian kingdom in the [[event:1132|Sima Qian's Records]], noting that a Han envoy found it a substantial state. [[event:1127|Emperor Wu of Han]] subjugated the Dian around 109 BC and reportedly allowed its king to keep his gold seal of authority. Archaeological excavations at Shizhaishan have recovered thousands of artifacts providing an unparalleled window into a culture that left no written records of its own.
